feedback.text: I have recently came to across the Digital Fairness Act and found it to be a big step towards the consumer protection in the digital world and protecting consumers from the egregious practices that are currently widely employed by various companies. As someone who has been playing video games for the last 27 years, I would like to add some feedback that I deem to be an important part of the digital consumer market- video games. Sincegloballythe video game industry is bigger than the film, television, and music industries combined, I believe it deserves to be included in initiatives such as this one in a broader way. While stopping the gambling-like practices in video games is a good start, I do not believe it is enough. At the moment, buying a video game, be it digitally or sometimes even physically, does not guarantee that the buyer will have access to that game in the future. Some video games are shut down not even a few months after launch, some are shut down after a few years but always with very little notice. Prior to the popularisation of digital distribution and live service practices, buying a video game was akin to buying a music Vinyl/Cassette/CD or a VHS/DVD/BluRay of a film. Once purchased, you can access the content for as long as the medium physically allows (i.e. physical degradation) and as long as you have the device necessary to play that medium. This is, for the most part, no longer the case. Video game publishers, depending on a game, can simply cease the support of the game and said game will no longer be accessible. Recent examples of this are The Crew, Anthem, Counter-Strike: Global Offensive (free but with in-game purchases), Overwatch, Suicide Squad: Kill the Justice League, Babylon's Fall and, I am sure, quite a few more. Some of the games had millions of players, some dwindling yet loyal playerbases. Some of these game were switched to a new version of the game and others were shut down completely. Most all of them are no longer accessible as they were on launch. Now the aforementioned games are multiplayer games that require constant internet connection and access to the servers. However, a major part of modern big budget single player games now require internet connection as well, meaning in the even of the publisher closing or simply deciding so, access to that game can be terminated, leaving the buyer without the money paid or the product purchased. Similarly, most digital distribution platforms tie the games sold on their service to said service (such as Steam, Epic Games Launcher, Xbox, etc.) meaning that if the service shuts down, the content may be forever lost. As such, I believe it is crucial to introduce regulation to curb such practices in the future. A current EU citizen's initiative "Stop Destroying Videogames" is trying to tackle this issue and raise awareness of the anti-consumer practices used by the major video game publishers and explains this issue in more detail.
